static.description: A polyglot microservices platform for food delivery, composed of 5 independent services (API Gateway, Order, Inventory, Driver, Notification) across Kotlin, Java, Python, Go, and TypeScript stacks, orchestrated via Docker Compose with RabbitMQ as the async event bus.
domain_concepts: OrderStatus: Enum representing the lifecycle states of an order: PENDING → ACCEPTED → PREPARING → READY_FOR_PICKUP → IN_TRANSIT → DELIVERED, with CANCELLED and REFUNDED as terminal/side states.
Order: JPA entity representing a customer's food order, including line items, delivery address, total amount, Stripe PaymentIntent ID, and status lifecycle tracking.
OrderItem: Embeddable value object within an Order representing a single line item with menu item reference, name, quantity, and unit price.

MenuItem: SQLAlchemy model for a restaurant menu item with stock tracking; stock_quantity of -1 indicates unlimited availability, otherwise finite stock requiring Redis distributed locks for reservation.
Restaurant: SQLAlchemy model representing a restaurant entity with name, address, cuisine type, rating, and associated menu items.
Driver: MongoDB document representing a delivery driver with profile info, availability status, and GPS coordinates.
GeoPoint: Embedded struct in the Driver document storing latitude and longitude for real-time location tracking.
JwtAuthFilter: Spring Cloud Gateway global filter that validates JWT tokens, extracts the customer ID from the 'sub' claim, and forwards it as the X-Customer-Id header to downstream services.
order.events: RabbitMQ exchange used for publishing order lifecycle events (e.g., order.created, order status changes) consumed by the Notification Service.
acquire_lock: Redis-based distributed lock function in the Inventory Service that prevents concurrent stock overselling by acquiring an exclusive lock per menu item with TTL and retry logic.
isValidTransition: Static method on OrderStatus that enforces the allowed state machine transitions for the order lifecycle.
stripePaymentIntentId: Stripe PaymentIntent ID (pi_xxxx) stored on the Order, used for manual capture of payments and refund operations.

dynamic.architecture_summary: The system follows a microservices architecture with an API Gateway (Kotlin/Spring Cloud Gateway) that performs JWT authentication and routes requests to three backend services: Order Service (Java/Spring Boot with PostgreSQL and Stripe payments), Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI with PostgreSQL and Redis distributed locks), and Driver Service (Go with MongoDB). RabbitMQ serves as the async event bus for order lifecycle events, consumed by a Notification Service (Node.js/TypeScript) that dispatches SMS via Twilio and emails via SendGrid.

key_components.name: GatewayApplication
key_components.responsibility: Spring Cloud Gateway entry point that routes external HTTP requests to internal microservices.

key_components.name: JwtAuthFilter
key_components.responsibility: Global gateway filter that validates JWT tokens and injects the authenticated customer ID as a downstream header.

key_components.name: OrderServiceApplication
key_components.responsibility: Spring Boot entry point for the Order Service handling order CRUD and lifecycle management.

key_components.name: OrderController
key_components.responsibility: REST controller exposing endpoints for creating, listing, retrieving, updating, and cancelling orders.

key_components.name: OrderService
key_components.responsibility: Business logic layer that manages order persistence, Stripe payment intents, status transitions, and RabbitMQ event publishing.

key_components.name: Order
key_components.responsibility: JPA entity representing a food delivery order with embedded line items, payment tracking, and audit timestamps.

key_components.name: OrderStatus
key_components.responsibility: Enum defining the order lifecycle state machine and valid state transitions.

key_components.name: Inventory Service main
key_components.responsibility: FastAPI application handling restaurant/menu queries and stock reservation using Redis distributed locks.

key_components.name: Inventory models
key_components.responsibility: SQLAlchemy ORM models for Restaurant and MenuItem entities with stock management.

key_components.name: Driver Service main
key_components.responsibility: Go HTTP server providing driver profile CRUD and location tracking backed by MongoDB with graceful shutdown.

key_components.name: Notification Service
key_components.responsibility: TypeScript service consuming RabbitMQ order events and dispatching SMS (Twilio) and email (SendGrid) notifications with idempotency guards.

agent_safe_operations: Add new REST endpoints to existing controllers/routers in any service
Add new unit or integration tests to any service
Modify business logic in OrderService, inventory main.py, or driver main.go
Add new domain models or DTOs within existing service source directories
Update application configuration files (application.yml, application.properties, requirements.txt, package.json, pom.xml, build.gradle.kts) for non-secret values
Add new RabbitMQ event consumers or producers within existing services
Refactor existing service code for readability or performance
Add new utility/helper modules within any service's source directory
agent_forbidden_operations: Modify or create .env files containing real secrets
Change the openapi.yaml specification without coordinating across all services
Alter CI/CD workflow files without explicit approval
Modify database migration files or schema definitions that could cause data loss
Change JWT secret configuration or authentication bypass logic
Commit Stripe, Twilio, or SendGrid API keys or any real credentials to source code
Remove or weaken the OrderStatus.isValidTransition state machine constraints
potential_pitfalls: Each service uses a different language and build tool — changes must respect the correct build system (Maven for order-service, Gradle for api-gateway, pip for inventory-service, go modules for driver-service, npm for notification-service).
The inventory-service uses async SQLAlchemy with asyncpg — all database operations must use async/await patterns and the AsyncSession dependency.
OrderStatus has a strict state machine enforced by isValidTransition(); adding new statuses requires updating all switch cases to avoid compilation errors.
The Order entity uses @ElementCollection for OrderItem which creates a separate order_items table — bulk operations on items can cause N+1 queries if not handled carefully.
The API Gateway's JwtAuthFilter has PUBLIC_PATHS that bypass auth — forgetting to add new public endpoints there will cause 401 errors.
The inventory service uses Redis distributed locks for stock reservation — tests that modify stock must account for Redis availability or mock the lock functions.
The driver-service is plain Go net/http with manual routing (no framework) — new endpoints require updating the ServeHTTP switch statement.
RabbitMQ exchange 'order.events' uses topic routing (order.*) — new event types must follow the 'order.<action>' naming pattern to be routed correctly.
The notification service uses an in-memory Set for idempotency — this is lost on restart; production-critical changes should consider Redis-backed idempotency.
Stripe integration uses MANUAL capture method — orders require explicit payment capture after acceptance, and refunds reference the PaymentIntent ID stored on the order.
